retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anion Gap -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: To best understand the concept of anion gap, let us revisit the law of electrochemical neutrality. This law states that for any given solution, the net charge after accounting for all cations (positive ions) and anions (negative ions) should arrive at zero. However, at face value, the subtraction between the measured cations and measured anions of the blood does not yield a zero; the 4 to 12 mmol/L range for “normal anion gap.” However, this does not reflect an integral problem in the mathematical derivations, but rather its paramount utility. The resulting positive number in a healthy, asymptomatic patient is a result of the formula accounting only for measured cations and anions, leaving uncalculated cations and uncalculated anions to reach the zero value.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Blood is collected in lithium heparin tubes and then goes to the laboratory to evaluate serum electrolytes. [18] The collection tubes should not be left for an extended period as cell lysis can occur, causing the intracellular electrolytes and other contents to come out in the serum.

retrieved_docs.title: International Normalized Ratio: Assessment, Monitoring, and Clinical Implications -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: In 2017, the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute recommended that blood specimens for PT and INR testing in the laboratory setting should be obtained through intravenous extraction and collected directly into a tube with a light blue top. This tube contains 3.2% sodium citrate as the anticoagulant. [12] The tubes must be filled to at least 90% of their total capacity. The tube should be gently inverted several times immediately after collection to ensure proper mixing of blood and anticoagulant. The total time between sample collection and testing should not exceed 24 h. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anion Gap -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: In essence, the anion gap is a mathematical calculation that provides practitioners with the foresight to plan for managing current problems associated with acid-base balance, fluids, and electrolytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: For blood phosphate estimation, serum or plasma is an option. For plasma, lithium heparin is the agent of choice as anticoagulants such as EDTA, oxalate, and citrate may interfere with the formation of the phosphomolybdate complex in routine assays.

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudohyponatremia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Indirect ion-selective electrode potentiometry: In the 1980s, laboratories began transitioning to a new method called indirect ion-selective electrode potentiometry. This laboratory method provides an advantage over flame emission spectrophotometry in that only the aqueous components of the solution are measured. However, the serum in the indirect ion-selective electrode is again diluted, which, in turn, requires a corrective factor. Thus, the indirect ion-selective electrode method of sodium measurement is also prone to error in the presence of excess solute in the serum when considering the percentage of the aqueous component. According to a recent participant summary report conducted by the College of American Pathologists, approximately two-thirds of laboratories in the United States currently utilize this method.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Bicarbonate
retrieved_docs.content: Normal range: 23 to 30 mmol/L It increases or decreases depending on the acid-base status.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperchloremia: excessive normal saline infusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Testing Procedures -- Titration
retrieved_docs.content: It is a procedure used to identify the quantity of acid or base in a solution with an indicator's help. It helps find an estimate of acids and bases in body fluids such as gastric juice HCl.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anion Gap -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The anion gap is used to identify errors in the measurement of electrolytes (sodium, chloride, bicarbonate, and potassium most notably), detect paraproteins (IgG for example), and evaluate for suspected acid-base disorders – the latter being the most common and essential use.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hypochloremia: increased gastrointestinal or renal losses.

retrieved_docs.title: Pseudohyponatremia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Direct ion-selective electrode potentiometry: Similar to the indirect ion-selective electrode, the direct ion-selective electrode potentiometry method also utilizes potentiometry to precisely detect sodium levels in a given serum sample. However, the direct method does not require serum dilution before testing. The reported serum sodium level does not require a correction factor and is therefore not susceptible to an artifactual error by excess solute in serum. Direct ion-selective electrode potentiometry is the most frequently used method in arterial blood gas analysis. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrolytes -- Specimen Collection
retrieved_docs.content: A blood specimen for electrolytes uses lithium heparin tubes, plus the standard phlebotomy equipment and personnel, as with any blood draw. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Biomarkers -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Serum or plasma may be used to measure aspartate aminotransferase (AST). Heparin, oxalate, EDTA, and citrate do not cause enzyme inhibition. Anticoagulants with ammonium as the cation should be avoided to reduce the possibility of error. Because of the high activity of AST in red blood cells, hemolyzed samples are unacceptable. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophysiologic Study Interpretation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The patient must be fasting for at least 6 hours before the procedure. Antiarrhythmic therapy must be withheld for at least 4 half-lives. Hypoglycemic therapy must be appropriately adjusted. Anticoagulation therapy is usually interrupted before the procedure for 3 to 4 days for warfarin and 1 to 2 days for novel anticoagulant regimens. "Bridging therapy" with low molecular weight heparin is generally not indicated. [7] On the other hand, uninterrupted therapy is an option with the novel anticoagulants. [8] However, the approach should be individualized, considering the patient's thromboembolic and bleeding risk. Intravenous access should be secured before arrival in the EP laboratory, and fluids should be administered during the procedure to avoid dehydration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperosmolality and hypernatriemia are classified as part of the broader category of other disorders of fluid, electrolyte, and acid‑base balance, reflecting their role as disturbances in plasma osmolality and sodium concentration that impact overall homeostatic regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2548
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are intravenous solutions used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: irrigating solutions and electrolyte‑balancing formulations are organized within a unified pharmacological framework, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous therapies for fluid resuscitation, nutrition, electrolyte management, antifibrinolytic treatment and metabolic modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: clarifies how sodium‑based compounds, amino‑acid additives, antifibrinolytic agents, and irrigating solutions are interrelated and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate intravenous or irrigating formulations for fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are classified as members of the electrolyte group, indicating that they involve substances that influence electrolyte homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium ion and potassium supplements are directly linked, as potassium supplements are formulations designed to provide potassium ion to the body. By delivering potassium ion, these supplements help correct potassium deficiencies, support normal electrolyte balance, and maintain proper cellular function, making them an important therapeutic option in the management of conditions such as hypokalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى (renal failure), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals suffering from kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2889
retrieved_docs.content: Chromic chloride is classified within the category of electrolyte/mineral replacement, indicating that it functions as a mineral or electrolyte supplement used in therapeutic replacement regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2594
retrieved_docs.content: The amiloride hydrochloride 5 MG / bumetanide 1 MG oral tablet fulfills the potassium‑sparing diuretic therapeutic role, providing diuretic action while helping to preserve potassium balance in patients who require fluid removal without excessive potassium loss.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 286
retrieved_docs.content: زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية (increased anion gap) is clinically associated with قصور الكلى (renal failure), indicating that patients with renal failure often exhibit a widened anion gap in laboratory assessments. Consequently, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in individuals with قصور الكلى, meaning that the use of magnesium gluconate should be avoided in patients suffering from renal failure to prevent potential adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
retrieved_docs.content: form a subset, with specific formulations such as CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES classified under “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance.” This hierarchical organization clarifies how various irrigating, perfusion, electrolyte‑balancing, and blood‑substitute products are related and categorized for clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 144
retrieved_docs.content: among these drug classes. Other hematological agents are also part of the blood and blood‑forming organ drugs class, as are hemostatic agents. The hemostatic effects category includes vitamin K and other hemostatics, which are specialized agents used to promote coagulation. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that antianemic preparations, various iron‑based products, folic acid/vitamin B12, and other hematological agents are integrated under blood and blood‑forming organ drugs, while blood substitutes, perfusion solutions, irrigating fluids, and electrolyte‑balancing intravenous preparations constitute a related but distinct subgroup focused on fluid and electrolyte management, with specific members such as glycine, sodium bicarbonate, and ferrous ascorbate illustrating concrete examples of these classifications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: cardioplegic electrolyte additives are integrated within a structured pharmacologic framework that supports fluid‑electrolyte management, organ protection, and therapeutic interventions in cardiac surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Amiloride is classified within the ATC group “Other potassium‑sparing agents,” and increased diuresis is a physiologic effect of amiloride, reflecting its ability to promote urine output while conserving potassium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchy of intravenous and irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and related pharmacologic agents, emphasizing their therapeutic indications, contraindications, and connections to thromboembolic and shock states. CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is classified within Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance and is used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients; it is also grouped with antidiarrheals and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting combined electrolyte replacement and gastrointestinal therapeutic roles. BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS is a high‑level category that includes irrigating solutions, blood substitutes, and perfusion products, serving as the parent class for both “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” and “IV solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients,” and it subsumes the broader group blood and related products.
